sense_from: the grove and gymnasium Akademeia northwest of Athens, sacred to Athena and named after the hero Akademos
sense_to: an institution of higher learning or scholarship; a society of scholars, scientists, or artists
trigger_label: Plato founds his school in the grove Akademeia
trigger_desc: Around 387 BC Plato established his philosophical school in the grove Akademeia outside Athens. The school took the name of the place, and the place name thereby came to denote an institution of learning, the meaning carried into Latin academia and the modern languages.

sense_from: having no alternative; describing a situation where no other course of action is available
sense_to: a political rhetoric device that falsely frames a debatable decision as the only possible option, suppressing discussion; widely mocked and criticised
trigger_label: Angela Merkel's repeated use of 'alternativlos' 2009-2010
trigger_desc: Chancellor Merkel systematically used 'alternativlos' to justify the Euro rescue package, health reform, and Stuttgart 21, prompting the Gesellschaft fuer deutsche Sprache to name it Unwort des Jahres 2010.

sense_from: the surname of the French physicist and mathematician Andre-Marie Ampere
sense_to: the SI base unit of electric current; commonly clipped to 'amp'
trigger_label: Ampere founds electrodynamics
trigger_desc: From 1820 Andre-Marie Ampere formulated the laws relating electric current and magnetism, founding electrodynamics. His name was adopted for the unit of current at the 1881 International Electrical Congress.

sense_from: toil, hardship, suffering; forced drudgery; associated with servitude and pain
sense_to: work, labour; productive activity; a profession or occupation; a neutral or positive economic category
trigger_label: Protestant Reformation and the valorisation of secular vocation (Beruf)
trigger_desc: Luther's concept of the Beruf (calling/vocation) and the Protestant ethic elevated secular labour to a morally dignified activity, helping transform Arbeit from mere toil to valued work.

sense_from: Bayer's proprietary brand name for acetylsalicylic acid as a pharmaceutical product
sense_to: acetylsalicylic acid as a generic drug; loosely any common analgesic tablet
trigger_label: Bayer launches Aspirin brand
trigger_desc: In 1899 Bayer registered and marketed acetylsalicylic acid under the brand Aspirin. In several countries the trademark lapsed (in the United States it was ruled generic in 1921), and the brand name passed into common use as the name of the substance itself.

sense_from: a member of the Nizari Ismaili order of Syria and Persia, known to the Crusaders as the Assassins
sense_to: one who kills, especially a prominent person, by surprise attack, often for political reasons or for hire
trigger_label: Crusader contact with the Nizari Ismailis
trigger_desc: During the Crusades the Nizari Ismaili order of Syria and Persia (active from c. 1090) became known to European chroniclers under the Arabic pejorative hashishin. Their reputation for targeted killing of political and military figures attached the killing sense to the name; later legend, popularised by Marco Polo, reinforced it.

sense_from: Atlas, the Titan of Greek myth condemned to hold up the sky or the heavens on his shoulders
sense_to: a bound collection of maps; by extension a volume of systematic tables, charts, or illustrations on a given subject
trigger_label: Mercator's 'Atlas' map collection (1595)
trigger_desc: Gerardus Mercator titled his posthumously completed 1595 collection of maps 'Atlas', invoking the Titan who bore the heavens, and its frontispiece depicted the figure. The work's fame fixed 'atlas' as the common word for a bound book of maps, an English usage attested from 1636.

sense_from: an act of setting upon someone or something with force; a military or physical assault
sense_to: a sudden onset more generally: a bout of illness (e.g. heart attack), an offensive move in sport or a game, or a forceful piece of criticism
trigger_label: gradual polysemous extension of 'attack' across the 20th century
trigger_desc: No single datable event. Across the 20th century 'attack' extended from military/physical assault into medical, sporting and figurative domains. The cause is gradual polysemy and is undetermined; recorded here as a diffuse trigger so the change can be aligned with the SemEval signal without asserting a specific cause.

sense_from: a small fish suitable only for frying rather than for the table whole; later, fried fish
sense_to: an adolescent girl roughly fourteen to seventeen; a teenage girl no longer a child but not yet a woman
trigger_label: 19th c. student and literary usage, the Backfischroman
trigger_desc: Through 19th c. student slang and popular literature the word 'Backfisch' settled on the sense 'adolescent girl'. The genre of the 'Backfischroman' for young female readers spread the figurative sense across the German-speaking public in the later 19th century.

sense_from: Leo Baekeland's brand name for his phenol-formaldehyde synthetic resin
sense_to: loosely, any hard moulded phenolic plastic of the early-twentieth-century type
trigger_label: Baekeland markets Bakelite resin
trigger_desc: In 1909 Leo Baekeland announced and branded Bakelite, the first fully synthetic plastic. The brand became widely used as a loose generic label for hard moulded phenolic plastics of the era.

sense_from: a spherical or roughly round object, especially one used in a game or sport
sense_to: extended and figurative uses: a formal social dance; informally a very enjoyable time (to have a ball); idiomatic alertness (on the ball)
trigger_label: gradual polysemous extension of 'ball' across the 20th century
trigger_desc: No single datable event. The distribution of 'ball' across its object, social-dance and figurative senses shifted gradually; the cause is undetermined polysemy. Recorded as a diffuse trigger so the change can be aligned with the SemEval signal without asserting a specific cause.

sense_from: from Greek banausos, a manual worker or artisan; one who works with the hands rather than the mind
sense_to: a person without taste, sensibility, or appreciation for art and intellectual things; a cultural philistine
trigger_label: Neo-humanist cult of Bildung, late 18th to 19th century
trigger_desc: The neo-humanist revival of Greek learning and the 19th c. ideal of Bildung made the Greek 'banausos' a learned term of contempt. In German educated usage 'Banause' narrowed to a person without taste or appreciation for art and the intellect.

sense_from: (older slang, derogatory) behaving like a 'basehead', i.e. a crack-cocaine user
sense_to: (internet slang, approving) admirably true to oneself and unconcerned with others' approval; agreeably bold or unapologetic
trigger_label: Lil B's reappropriation of 'based' (c. 2010)
trigger_desc: Around 2010 the rapper Lil B redefined 'based', previously a drug-related slur, to mean being positive and true to oneself; internet communities then spread it as a term of approval.

sense_from: Bikini Atoll in the Marshall Islands, site of United States nuclear weapon tests from 1946
sense_to: a brief two-piece swimsuit for women
trigger_label: Operation Crossroads nuclear tests at Bikini Atoll
trigger_desc: The United States conducted nuclear tests at Bikini Atoll in July 1946. Designer Louis Reard named his new swimsuit 'bikini' the same month, trading on the explosive notoriety of the place name.

sense_from: fair, equitable, just, proper; reasonable and appropriate (as in 'recht und billig')
sense_to: cheap, of low quality, shoddy; low-priced and therefore presumed inferior
trigger_label: Pejoration ueber die Preisbedeutung
trigger_desc: Kein einzelnes datierbares Ereignis. Ueber die Zwischenbedeutung 'preiswert' kippte 'billig' zur negativen Wertung 'minderwertig', als niedriger Preis mit geringer Qualitaet gleichgesetzt wurde. Das Ankerjahr ist eine repraesentative Belegstelle, keine Ursache.

sense_from: the Biro brand of ballpoint pen, named after inventor Laszlo Biro
sense_to: any ballpoint pen (chiefly British and Commonwealth English)
trigger_label: Laszlo Biro patents the ballpoint pen
trigger_desc: Laszlo Biro patented his ballpoint pen in 1938, and pens were marketed under the Biro name. In Britain and the Commonwealth the brand name became the ordinary word for any ballpoint pen.

sense_from: a small piece, portion or quantity of something; a short while
sense_to: in computing, a binary digit; the smallest unit of information, taking the value 0 or 1
trigger_label: coining of 'bit' (binary digit) and the rise of digital computing
trigger_desc: The term 'bit', a contraction of 'binary digit' attributed to John W. Tukey, appeared in print in Claude Shannon's 1948 paper 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication'. The subsequent spread of digital computing established the computing sense, broadening the word from 'a small piece' into a technical unit of information.

sense_from: a bubble: a sphere of gas enclosed in a liquid
sense_to: an economic bubble: a period of unsustainable speculative asset prices that eventually bursts
trigger_label: the South Sea Bubble financial crisis (1720)
trigger_desc: The 1720 collapse of the South Sea Company's inflated share price, named the 'South Sea Bubble', fixed 'bubble' as the standard metaphor for a speculative market boom that bursts.

sense_from: an intensive aerial bombardment, especially the German bombing of Britain in 1940-1941 ('the Blitz')
sense_to: any sudden, concentrated, intensive effort or campaign (a media blitz, an advertising blitz)
trigger_label: The Blitz: German bombing campaign against Britain
trigger_desc: From September 1940 the German aerial bombing of Britain was named 'the Blitz' in English, clipped from 'Blitzkrieg'. The term then broadened to any sudden, intensive concentrated effort.

sense_from: after Thomas Bowdler (1754-1825), editor of the 1818 'Family Shakespeare' from which matter judged unfit was removed
sense_to: to remove from a text material considered improper or offensive, often to its detriment; to expurgate
trigger_label: Thomas Bowdler's 'Family Shakespeare' (1818)
trigger_desc: In 1818 Thomas Bowdler published an expurgated 'Family Shakespeare' with passages deemed indecent removed. His name became a verb (attested 1836) for the practice of purging text of supposedly offensive content.

sense_from: proper name of Captain Charles C. Boycott (1832-1897), English land agent in County Mayo
sense_to: organized refusal to deal with a person, business, or country as a means of coercion or protest
trigger_label: Irish Land League ostracism of Captain Boycott
trigger_desc: In 1880 the Irish Land League organized a campaign of social and economic isolation against land agent Charles Boycott after he refused to lower rents. The action drew international press coverage and his surname became the common term for the tactic.

sense_from: the surname of the French educator Louis Braille, who was himself blind
sense_to: a tactile writing system of raised dots read by touch; the everyday word for reading and writing used by blind people
trigger_label: Braille devises the raised-dot system
trigger_desc: In 1824 the fifteen-year-old Louis Braille devised a six-dot tactile code for reading and writing, published in 1829. The system spread internationally and took his name.

sense_from: savagely cruel, violent, beastly; like a brute, lacking human feeling
sense_to: as a colloquial intensifier: extremely harsh, intense or demanding; (slang) impressively intense, in an admiring way
trigger_label: Colloquial bleaching of a cruelty word into an intensifier
trigger_desc: No single datable event. Like 'savage' and 'killer', 'brutal' weakened in 20th-century colloquial speech from literal cruelty to a generic, sometimes admiring intensifier of intensity. The anchor year is a representative attestation of the intensifier use, not a cause.

sense_from: a thin sphere of liquid enclosing air or gas; a small body of gas in a liquid
sense_to: a period of trade or speculation in unsound or unsustainable ventures; inflated asset prices destined to collapse
trigger_label: the South Sea Bubble financial crisis (1720)
trigger_desc: The 1720 collapse of the South Sea Company's inflated share price, named the 'South Sea Bubble', fixed 'bubble' as the standard metaphor for a speculative market boom that bursts.

sense_from: to call off or annul an event, arrangement, or subscription; to mark with crossed lines
sense_to: to withdraw public, social, or commercial support from a person (especially a public figure) over objectionable conduct or speech
trigger_label: Social-media call-out culture and #MeToo (2017)
trigger_desc: The 2017 surge of social-media accountability movements, especially #MeToo, popularised 'cancelling' public figures, fixing the new transitive sense of 'to cancel someone'.

sense_from: the trademarked transparent regenerated-cellulose film commercialised by Brandenberger
sense_to: any thin transparent cellulose wrapping film
trigger_label: Cellophane brand film commercialised
trigger_desc: Jacques Brandenberger registered Cellophane and began commercial production of the transparent cellulose film around 1912. The brand became the ordinary word for the film, and a US court ruled it generic in 1936.

sense_from: from Nicolas Chauvin, a soldier of Napoleon I depicted as filled with uncritical devotion to the emperor
sense_to: aggressive, exaggerated nationalism or group loyalty; uncritical belief in the superiority of one's own nation or group
trigger_label: Cogniard brothers' play 'La cocarde tricolore' (1831)
trigger_desc: The 1831 vaudeville 'La cocarde tricolore' by the Cogniard brothers popularized the figure of Chauvin as the archetype of bellicose, boastful patriotism. From this the French 'chauvinisme' was coined (1843) and Latinized into German as 'Chauvinismus'.

sense_from: a round plane figure or ring; any roughly circular shape or arrangement
sense_to: a group of people connected by shared interest, acquaintance or activity (a circle of friends, literary circles, inner circle)
trigger_label: gradual polysemous extension of 'circle' across the 20th century
trigger_desc: No single datable event. The social-group sense of 'circle' gained relative weight gradually; the cause is undetermined polysemy. Recorded as a diffuse trigger so the change can be aligned with the SemEval signal without asserting a specific cause.

sense_from: a visible mass of condensed water vapour floating in the atmosphere
sense_to: cloud computing: remote networked computing resources (servers, storage, software) accessed via the internet, often depicted as a cloud in diagrams
trigger_label: Amazon Web Services launch and popularisation of cloud computing (2006)
trigger_desc: Amazon's launch of AWS EC2 and S3 in 2006, followed by Eric Schmidt's use of 'cloud computing' at the 2006 Search Engine Strategies conference, anchored the term in mainstream technology discourse.

sense_from: the city of Cologne on the Rhine, where the scented eau de Cologne was first produced
sense_to: a light perfume of alcohol and aromatic oils; eau de Cologne
trigger_label: Production of eau de Cologne
trigger_desc: From 1709 a scented water marketed as 'eau de Cologne' was produced in the city. Its fame fixed the city name as the English word for a light perfume.

sense_from: a Croat; specifically the 17th-century light cavalry who wore distinctive linen neck scarves
sense_to: a band or scarf of cloth worn around the neck, an early form of the necktie
trigger_label: Croat cavalry in the Thirty Years War
trigger_desc: Croat (Crabat) light cavalry fighting for the Catholic League wore linen neck scarves. The fashion was imitated in France from the 1650s, and the ethnonym 'cravate' became the name of the garment.

sense_from: to bend or crouch in fear or servility; to wince or recoil in embarrassment (verb)
sense_to: (slang) so embarrassing or awkward as to cause discomfort; used predicatively as an adjective or noun (that's cringe)
trigger_label: Social-media meme culture (mid-2010s, r/cringe)
trigger_desc: Online meme and reaction culture, including the r/cringe subreddit (founded 2011) and the 'cringe compilation' video genre, reanalysed 'cringe' from a verb into a predicative adjective and noun for embarrassing content during the mid-2010s.

sense_from: raisin of Corinth; a small seedless dried grape exported from the Greek port of Corinth
sense_to: a small dried grape; also the small round berry of the genus Ribes, named by resemblance
trigger_label: Corinth raisin trade
trigger_desc: Small dried grapes were exported from Corinth as 'raisins of Corauntz'. The place name was clipped to 'currant', with the final -s reanalysed as a plural ending, and became the fruit name.

sense_from: associated with the Syrian city of Damascus, the source of the figured woven cloth
sense_to: a reversible figured fabric woven with a pattern visible on both sides
trigger_label: Figured cloth from Damascus
trigger_desc: The patterned figured silk traded from Damascus carried the city name. Through Italian Damasco it entered German as Damast from the late medieval period.

sense_from: serge de Nimes; a twilled serge cloth associated with the French city of Nimes
sense_to: a coarse durable twilled cotton fabric, typically with a blue warp, used for work clothes and jeans
trigger_label: Textile production at Nimes
trigger_desc: The serge cloth exported as 'serge de Nimes' carried the city name. English speakers clipped the phrase to 'de Nim' and reanalysed it as the fabric name 'denim'.

sense_from: surname of the engineer Rudolf Diesel (1858-1913)
sense_to: compression-ignition engine; by extension the fuel it burns and a vehicle driven by such an engine
trigger_label: Rudolf Diesel's engine patent
trigger_desc: Rudolf Diesel patented his compression-ignition engine (German patent filed 1892, granted 1893) and published the design in 1893. The engine and, later, its fuel were named after the inventor, turning his surname into a generic technical term.

sense_from: online-slang coinage (c. 2018) for endlessly scrolling through negative news on social media
sense_to: the compulsive consumption of large quantities of negative online news, widely recognised general vocabulary
trigger_label: COVID-19 pandemic
trigger_desc: The 2020 COVID-19 pandemic, with its constant stream of alarming news, drove an explosion in the use of 'doomscrolling'; Merriam-Webster added it to its watch list and dictionary in 2020.

sense_from: of or pertaining to Draco, the Athenian legislator, or to the severe written law code he is said to have produced around 621 BC
sense_to: excessively harsh or severe, especially of laws, rules, or penalties
trigger_label: Draco's Athenian law code (c. 621 BC)
trigger_desc: The Athenian lawgiver Draco issued the city's first written code around 621 BC, notorious for prescribing death for even minor offences. The proverbial severity of these laws gave English 'draconian', which generalized to mean any disproportionately harsh measure.

sense_from: the sharp cutting side of a blade; the outer limit or boundary of an object or area
sense_to: figurative uses: a competitive advantage (to have the edge); tense irritability (on edge, edgy); the leading front of innovation (the cutting edge)
trigger_label: gradual polysemous extension of 'edge' across the 20th century
trigger_desc: No single datable event. The figurative senses of 'edge' (advantage, tension, frontier of innovation) gained relative weight gradually; the cause is undetermined polysemy. Recorded as a diffuse trigger so the change can be aligned with the SemEval signal without asserting a specific cause.

sense_from: remarkably good, distinguished, eminent; standing out from the crowd in a positive way (Latin egregius, 'out of the flock')
sense_to: remarkably bad; flagrant, conspicuously offensive; outrageously bad in a way that stands out
trigger_label: Ironic reversal of a term of praise
trigger_desc: No single datable event. The positive 'distinguished' was applied ironically (egregiously bad), and the ironic, negative sense displaced the original within a few decades in the later 16th century. The anchor year is the earliest negative attestation, not a cause.

sense_from: Otis Elevator Company's trademark for its powered moving stairway
sense_to: any moving staircase; a powered set of stairs that carries people between floors
trigger_label: Otis markets the Escalator brand
trigger_desc: The Otis Elevator Company introduced and trademarked the moving stairway under the brand Escalator around 1900. Widespread use of the brand as the ordinary name for moving stairways led a 1950 trademark ruling to find the word generic.

sense_from: the supply and rear zone behind the front; a halting place or a day's march on a military route
sense_to: a stage, phase, or leg; a distinct section of a journey, race, or process
trigger_label: First World War mass military service 1914-1918
trigger_desc: The First World War (1914-1918) exposed millions of German soldiers to the military 'Etappe'. Demobilisation carried the word into civilian language, where it generalised to mean any stage or leg of a journey or process.

sense_from: the front part of a person's head, from forehead to chin
sense_to: extended and figurative uses: an outward surface or front (the face of a cliff, a clock face); public standing or dignity (to lose or save face); nominal worth (face value); the public representative of a cause
trigger_label: gradual polysemous extension of 'face' across the 20th century
trigger_desc: No single datable event. The figurative and surface senses of 'face' gained relative weight gradually; the cause is undetermined polysemy. Recorded as a diffuse trigger so the change can be aligned with the SemEval signal without asserting a specific cause.

sense_from: the political movement and regime founded by Benito Mussolini in Italy, from Italian 'fascio' (bundle, league)
sense_to: any authoritarian, ultranationalist, anti-democratic ideology or regime of the far right
trigger_label: Rise of Mussolini's Fasci and the March on Rome
trigger_desc: Mussolini founded the Fasci Italiani di Combattimento in 1919; the 1922 March on Rome brought the movement to power. English borrowed 'fascism' as its proper name, then broadened it to far-right authoritarianism generally.

sense_from: literal compound 'filter bubble' (technical/ad hoc), not an established term
sense_to: the state of intellectual isolation that can result from algorithmic personalisation showing users only agreeable content
trigger_label: Eli Pariser, 'The Filter Bubble' (2011)
trigger_desc: Eli Pariser's 2011 book 'The Filter Bubble' coined the concept; the German loan-translation 'Filterblase' became the standard term for algorithmic information isolation in German media debate.

sense_from: the Sanitas (later AEG) brand 'Foen' for an electric hair dryer
sense_to: any electric hair dryer, regardless of maker (colloquial German, often spelled Foehn)
trigger_label: Sanitas registers the Foen hair-dryer brand
trigger_desc: The company Sanitas registered the trademark Foen for its electric hair dryer in 1909 (first model 1908). After AEG acquired Sanitas the brand persisted, and the word became the ordinary German term for any hair dryer.

sense_from: Victor Frankenstein, the scientist in Mary Shelley's novel who assembles and animates a creature from dead matter
sense_to: a creation or agency that escapes the control of and ultimately destroys its maker; loosely (and popularly) the monstrous creature itself
trigger_label: Mary Shelley's 'Frankenstein' (1818)
trigger_desc: Mary Shelley's novel 'Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus' (1818) told of a scientist whose animated creature turns against him. The name generalized to mean a creation that overpowers and destroys its creator, and (by popular confusion) the creature itself, attested figuratively from the 1830s.

sense_from: noble lady, mistress of a house; a woman of high rank; an honorific title (MHG frouwe)
sense_to: any adult female person; a woman, regardless of rank; (also) wife
trigger_label: Soziale Generalisierung eines Standestitels
trigger_desc: Kein einzelnes datierbares Ereignis. 'Frau' verlor ueber Jahrhunderte den exklusiven Adelsbezug und wurde zur allgemeinen Bezeichnung jeder erwachsenen Frau, waehrend 'Weib' absank. Das Ankerjahr markiert die etablierte allgemeine Bedeutung, keine Ursache.

sense_from: capable, brave, useful, upright; someone competent and of good character; 'that profits me' (das frommt mir)
sense_to: pious, devout, god-fearing; devoted to religion; sometimes (in derivatives) mock-pious or sanctimonious
trigger_label: Luther's Bible translation 1522-1534
trigger_desc: Luther's translation systematically used 'fromm' for the biblical concept of God-fearing righteousness, transposing the word into the religious sphere and displacing its earlier general sense of capability.

sense_from: the foremost line or face of a drawn-up military formation; the side a unit faces
sense_to: the front line as the zone of combat; the contested line of confrontation, also figuratively (political front, weather front)
trigger_label: First World War static trench warfare 1914-1918
trigger_desc: The static line warfare of the First World War (1914-1918) fixed 'die Front' as the term for the combat zone in everyday German. From this war usage the word broadened to any line of confrontation, political or meteorological.

sense_from: a strong, musty, unpleasant smell; body odour
sense_to: earthy, soulful, danceable quality; the funk music genre; (adj. funky) stylish, excellent
trigger_label: emergence of funk music
trigger_desc: The rise of funk as an African-American music genre (James Brown et al.) carried the word from 'bad smell' to a marker of style and soul.

sense_from: derived from the surname of the Italian physician Luigi Galvani; literally to treat with an electric current
sense_to: to electroplate or zinc-coat metal; figuratively, to rouse or energize
trigger_label: Galvani's bioelectricity experiments
trigger_desc: Around 1791 Luigi Galvani published experiments on the twitching of frog-leg muscles under electric stimulation. The German verb formed from his name first meant to treat with current, then broadened to electroplating and figurative rousing.

sense_from: derived from the surname of the Italian physician Luigi Galvani; literally to stimulate muscle by an electric current
sense_to: to shock or rouse a person or group into sudden, energetic action; also to coat iron or steel with zinc
trigger_label: Galvani's bioelectricity experiments
trigger_desc: Around 1791 Luigi Galvani published experiments showing that frog-leg muscles twitch when touched by metal, which he attributed to 'animal electricity'. The verb derived from his name first meant to stimulate by current, then broadened to mean rousing into action.

sense_from: of or pertaining to Gargantua, the voracious giant of Rabelais's satirical romance
sense_to: enormous; of tremendous size, volume, or extent, especially of appetite, quantity, or scale
trigger_label: Rabelais's 'Gargantua' (1534)
trigger_desc: Francois Rabelais's comic novel 'La Vie tres horrifique du grand Gargantua' (1534) centred on a giant of prodigious size and appetite. The character's fame made 'gargantuan' an English adjective for the enormous, attested from the 1590s.

sense_from: psychological manipulation that makes a victim doubt their own memory, perception, or sanity
sense_to: (broadened popular use) any grossly misleading, deceptive, or manipulative claim, especially in politics and public discourse
trigger_label: Political and media discourse surge (2016-2018)
trigger_desc: From the 2016 US election onward, commentators applied 'gaslighting' to political messaging and misinformation, broadening it from a clinical term to a general label for deceptive manipulation; Merriam-Webster reported a steep lookup and usage rise.

sense_from: joyful, carefree, light-hearted; bright and showy (of colour or dress)
sense_to: homosexual; relating to gay people or gay culture (neutral to positive after reappropriation)
trigger_label: Stonewall Riots and gay liberation movement
trigger_desc: The 1969 Stonewall Riots and subsequent gay liberation movement accelerated the reappropriation of 'gay' as a neutral/positive self-identifier.

sense_from: lascivious, lewd, lustful; sexually aroused
sense_to: great, awesome, fantastic; colloquial expression of strong approval
trigger_label: West German youth slang of the 1970s-80s
trigger_desc: West German youth culture of the 1970s repurposed 'geil' as an intensifier of approval, analogous to English 'wicked' or 'sick'. The Veltins beer slogan 'Geil ist geil' (1990s) anchored the shift in mainstream usage.

sense_from: 'Sternchen': a little star; the asterisk typographic symbol; informally a young female celebrity
sense_to: the gender asterisk used in written German to denote all genders inclusively (e.g. Leser*innen)
trigger_label: German gender-inclusive language debate (2010s)
trigger_desc: The 2010s German debate over gender-inclusive language popularised the asterisk form (Leser*innen); 'Gendersternchen' was the Gesellschaft fuer deutsche Sprache 'Anglizismus/Wort'-shortlist term and entered the Duden in 2020.

sense_from: satirical 1812 coinage from Governor Elbridge Gerry plus 'salamander', naming a contorted Massachusetts electoral district
sense_to: to manipulate the boundaries of an electoral district so as to favour one party or group; the practice of doing so
trigger_label: Massachusetts redistricting under Governor Gerry
trigger_desc: In 1812 Massachusetts redrew its senatorial districts to favour Governor Elbridge Gerry's party. A press cartoon likened one contorted district to a salamander, and the blend 'Gerry-mander' entered the language as a generic term for partisan map manipulation.

sense_from: the appearance or activity of a ghost; a spectral haunting
sense_to: ending a personal relationship by suddenly and without explanation ceasing all communication
trigger_label: Mainstreaming of mobile dating apps (Tinder, 2012-)
trigger_desc: Swipe-based mobile dating apps such as Tinder (launched 2012) normalised low-friction matching and easy disappearance, and the term 'ghosting' for abrupt no-explanation withdrawal spread rapidly in 2015.

sense_from: a trademarked brand of baked whole-grain breakfast cereal
sense_to: any breakfast food of baked rolled oats with nuts, honey, and dried fruit
trigger_label: Granola registered as a cereal brand
trigger_desc: The name Granola was registered as a breakfast-cereal trademark around 1886 (a respelling of the earlier Granula). After the trademark lapsed the word passed into general use for any oat-based baked cereal.

sense_from: the colour between blue and yellow in the spectrum; the colour of growing foliage
sense_to: environmentally friendly; concerned with or supporting ecological protection and green politics
trigger_label: the modern environmental movement and the rise of Green politics
trigger_desc: The environmental movement of the 1970s-80s (Earth Day 1970, Greenpeace 1971, and the founding of Green parties) attached an ecological-political meaning to 'green', making it shorthand for environmentally friendly.

sense_from: the colour of growing foliage; green
sense_to: environmentally friendly; relating to ecological politics and the Green party
trigger_label: the modern environmental movement and the rise of Green politics
trigger_desc: The environmental movement of the 1970s-80s (Earth Day 1970, Greenpeace 1971, and the founding of Green parties) attached an ecological-political meaning to 'green', making it shorthand for environmentally friendly.

sense_from: the surname of Joseph-Ignace Guillotin, French physician and deputy
sense_to: a machine for beheading by a heavy weighted blade sliding in vertical grooves; by extension any device with a descending blade
trigger_label: Proposal and adoption of the beheading machine in the French Revolution
trigger_desc: In 1789 deputy Joseph-Ignace Guillotin proposed that capital punishment use a uniform mechanical device. The machine was adopted in 1792 during the French Revolution and was named after him by metonymy, despite his opposition to the eponym.

sense_from: a genuinely good person; someone with good intentions and moral character
sense_to: a self-righteous, naive moraliser who imposes political correctness on others; a do-gooder (derogatory)
trigger_label: Gutmensch chosen as Unwort des Jahres 2015
trigger_desc: In January 2016 a language-critical jury named Gutmensch the non-word of 2015, reflecting how the term had become a right-wing polemical weapon to dismiss liberal opponents.

sense_from: an effigy of Guy Fawkes burned on 5 November; by extension a grotesquely or oddly dressed person
sense_to: a man; a fellow; (in plural) people of any gender
trigger_label: The Gunpowder Plot of Guy Fawkes
trigger_desc: In 1605 Guy Fawkes was caught guarding explosives beneath the English Parliament in the Gunpowder Plot. The annual burning of his effigy ('a guy') on 5 November fixed his name to a grotesque figure, from which the broadened modern sense 'man, fellow' later developed.

sense_from: place of residence or domicile; the locality where one has settlement rights; a neutral legal-territorial term
sense_to: the place of one's roots and emotional belonging; an idealised concept of origin, landscape, and identity
trigger_label: German Romanticism and 19th c. nationalism
trigger_desc: From around 1800 the Romantic movement and the subsequent national awakening invested 'Heimat' with sentimental and ideological meaning, turning a legal-territorial term into a concept of emotional belonging and origin.

sense_from: of or pertaining to Hercules, the Greek and Roman hero famed for prodigious strength and the Twelve Labours
sense_to: requiring or showing immense strength, size, or effort; extremely difficult to accomplish
trigger_label: Myth of Hercules and the Twelve Labours
trigger_desc: Hercules (Greek Herakles) was the archetypal hero of superhuman strength, set the Twelve Labours as penance. His legend, transmitted through classical literature, made 'herculean' an adjective for any task of extraordinary difficulty or any feat of great power, attested in English around 1600.

sense_from: the surname of the German physicist Heinrich Rudolf Hertz
sense_to: the SI unit of frequency, one cycle per second; an everyday word for the frequency of signals, sound, or processors
trigger_label: Hertz demonstrates electromagnetic waves
trigger_desc: In 1888 Heinrich Hertz experimentally produced and detected electromagnetic (radio) waves, confirming Maxwell's theory. His name was adopted for the unit of frequency, formally ratified by the IEC in 1935 and the General Conference on Weights and Measures in 1960.

sense_from: a name, probably from the comic-Irish surname Houlihan, attached to rowdy London street youths in 1898 newspaper reports
sense_to: a violent, lawless young person; a member of a rowdy gang or troublemaker
trigger_label: London press police-court reports of summer 1898
trigger_desc: In the summer of 1898 London newspaper police-court reports popularized 'Hooligan', probably from the comic-Irish surname Houlihan, as the label for unruly street youths. The proper-name origin generalized rapidly into a common noun for any violent young troublemaker.

sense_from: a person who rejects vaccination, especially on principle (descriptive)
sense_to: a strongly politicised, frequently derogatory label for opponents of COVID-19 vaccination, associated with conspiracy and protest milieus
trigger_label: COVID-19 vaccination debate (2020-2021)
trigger_desc: The 2020-2021 public debate over COVID-19 vaccination sharply politicised 'Impfgegner', loading the descriptive term with a derogatory, conflict-laden charge; DWDS shows a steep frequency rise.

sense_from: a person or thing that influences another; one who exerts influence
sense_to: a person who has gained a large following on social media and is paid to promote products or lifestyles to that audience
trigger_label: Rise of Instagram and influencer marketing (mid-2010s)
trigger_desc: The growth of Instagram (launched 2010) and the surrounding creator economy turned 'influencer' into a job title for paid social-media promoters, narrowing the generic sense to this commercial meaning by the mid-2010s.

sense_from: a heavy twilled cotton or fustian cloth named for Genoa, the port from which it was traded
sense_to: casual trousers made of denim or similar hard-wearing cloth
trigger_label: Genoese cloth trade
trigger_desc: Genoa (French Genes) was the European source of a sturdy fustian cloth. The fabric carried the city name into English as 'jean'; the trousers cut from it later took the same name.

sense_from: the bellicose pro-war sentiment of the 'jingoes', from the refrain 'by jingo' in G. W. Hunt's 1878 music-hall song
sense_to: aggressive, boastful patriotism, especially favouring a belligerent foreign policy
trigger_label: G. W. Hunt's 1878 music-hall song during the Russo-Turkish crisis
trigger_desc: During the 1877-1878 crisis over British intervention against Russia, G. W. Hunt's music-hall song popularized by G. H. MacDermott carried the refrain 'we don't want to fight but by jingo if we do'. Supporters were dubbed 'jingoes' and 'jingoism' became a contemptuous label for belligerent patriotism.

sense_from: of or pertaining to the writer Franz Kafka or to his literary style
sense_to: marked by a nightmarish, oppressive, and senseless complexity, especially of impenetrable bureaucracy or illogical official process; absurd in a menacing, disorienting way
trigger_label: Posthumous publication of Kafka's novels ('Der Prozess', 1925)
trigger_desc: Franz Kafka's novels, including 'Der Prozess' (The Trial), published posthumously from 1925, depicted individuals trapped in opaque, irrational bureaucratic systems. As their reputation spread in the 1930s and 1940s, 'Kafkaesque' came to mean oppressively absurd, bureaucratic, and disorienting, beyond mere reference to the author.

sense_from: the Roman cognomen Caesar, the family name of Gaius Julius Caesar
sense_to: emperor; the supreme monarchic ruler, originally of the Roman empire and later of the Holy Roman and German empires
trigger_label: Establishment of the Roman imperial title under Augustus
trigger_desc: From 27 BC onward Octavian, the adopted heir of Julius Caesar, carried Caesar as part of his name and ruled as the first Roman emperor. His successors adopted Caesar as a standing element of the imperial titulature, converting the family name into a title of office; Germanic peoples borrowed it early as Kaisar/Kaiser.

sense_from: a Japanese suicide pilot or aircraft of the Second World War; the suicide-attack corps
sense_to: recklessly self-destructive; undertaken with no regard for one's own safety
trigger_label: Japanese kamikaze suicide attacks in the Pacific War
trigger_desc: Beginning at the Battle of Leyte Gulf in October 1944, organised Japanese suicide air attacks brought 'kamikaze' into English; the term later broadened to a figurative adjective for reckless self-destruction.

sense_from: a mounted nobleman or knight; a horseman of rank
sense_to: a man of polished, courteous, chivalrous manners, especially toward women; a gentleman
trigger_label: Baroque court culture and the Kavalierstour, 17th century
trigger_desc: Baroque court society and the educational grand tour of young nobles (the Kavalierstour) made the 'Kavalier' the model of polished courtly manners. The word broadened from a mounted noble to any man of courteous, chivalrous bearing.

sense_from: a member of the Cathar (Albigensian) sect; the specific dualist movement of southern France and northern Italy
sense_to: a heretic; any person whose religious teaching is condemned as deviant by the Church
trigger_label: Albigensian Crusade against the Cathars
trigger_desc: Pope Innocent III launched the Albigensian Crusade in 1209 to suppress the Cathar sect. The sustained inquisitorial campaign made the group name (Latin Cathari, German Katzer/Ketzer) the standard word for heretics in general.

sense_from: a category, division, or rank into which things or persons are grouped; also a school class
sense_to: a social class defined by its relation to the means of production; a key category of socialist and Marxist social analysis
trigger_label: 1848 socialist movement and Marx's Das Kapital (1867)
trigger_desc: The Communist Manifesto of 1848 and Marx's Das Kapital (1867) gave 'Klasse' a precise socio-economic meaning, the social class defined by its relation to the means of production. The socialist labour movement spread this technical sense in German.

sense_from: Kimberly-Clark's brand of disposable facial tissue
sense_to: any disposable paper tissue or handkerchief
trigger_label: Kimberly-Clark launches Kleenex
trigger_desc: Kimberly-Clark introduced Kleenex disposable facial tissue in 1924. The brand became so dominant that the name began to be used for any paper tissue, a usage the company has since fought to limit.

sense_from: a climate activist (Letzte Generation) who glues themselves to roads to block traffic; mocking derogatory label used by opponents
sense_to: a road-gluing climate activist (Letzte Generation); increasingly neutral self-designation and dictionary entry
trigger_label: Letzte Generation street-blockade campaign from January 2022
trigger_desc: From 24 January 2022 the Letzte Generation began gluing themselves to streets in Berlin. The Bild newspaper coined 'Klimakleber' as a pejorative. By 2023 activists began using the term themselves, initiating partial reappropriation.

sense_from: blatant, gross, glaring; extreme in a bad or shocking way
sense_to: (youth slang) impressive, amazing, intense; used approvingly as a general intensifier
trigger_label: Jugendsprachliche Aufwertung eines Negativworts
trigger_desc: Kein einzelnes datierbares Ereignis. In der Jugendsprache seit den 1980er Jahren wurde 'krass' vom negativen 'extrem schlimm' zu einem allgemeinen, oft positiven Verstaerker. Das Ankerjahr ist eine repraesentative Belegstelle, keine Ursache.

sense_from: Beiersdorf's brand of lip-care stick
sense_to: any lip-care stick, regardless of maker (colloquial German)
trigger_label: Beiersdorf introduces Labello
trigger_desc: Beiersdorf introduced the Labello lip-care stick in 1909. The brand became so dominant in German-speaking markets that the name is colloquially used for any lip balm.

sense_from: of or relating to Laconia or Lakedaimon, the region of Sparta in the Peloponnese
sense_to: using few words; terse and concise in speech or writing
trigger_label: Reputation of Laconian (Spartan) terseness
trigger_desc: Classical writers recorded the Laconians of Sparta as famously curt of speech. Renaissance humanists revived the adjective 'laconic' from the region name to mean terse expression.

sense_from: the Venetian quarantine island and its plague hospital on Santa Maria di Nazaret (the lazzaretto)
sense_to: a hospital for the isolation of the sick, especially a plague or military field hospital
trigger_label: Founding of the Venetian Lazzaretto Vecchio
trigger_desc: In 1423 Venice converted the island of Santa Maria di Nazaret into a permanent plague hospital and quarantine station, the Lazzaretto Vecchio. The island name (blended with St Lazarus) generalised into a common noun for an isolation hospital and spread into German as Lazarett.

sense_from: of or relating to the Greek island of Lesbos and its inhabitants
sense_to: of a woman, homosexual; relating to female same-sex desire
trigger_label: Sappho of Lesbos and her reception
trigger_desc: The poet Sappho of Lesbos wrote of love between women. Nineteenth-century reception of her verse turned the island adjective 'Lesbian' into a term for female same-sex desire.

sense_from: to find agreeable, pleasant, or satisfactory; to be fond of
sense_to: to register approval of a social-media post by clicking a Like button; the resulting endorsement
trigger_label: Facebook Like button launch (2009)
trigger_desc: Facebook launched its 'Like' button in February 2009; the feature spread across the web and social platforms, turning the everyday verb into a one-click social endorsement and a countable noun ('likes').

sense_from: to like (borrowed verb stem, before the social-media sense fixed)
sense_to: to like: to register approval of a social-media post via the Like button
trigger_label: Facebook Like button launch (2009)
trigger_desc: Facebook launched its 'Like' button in February 2009; the feature spread across the web and social platforms, turning the everyday verb into a one-click social endorsement and a countable noun ('likes').

sense_from: Frederick Walton's trademarked floor covering made from oxidised linseed oil
sense_to: any smooth floor covering of this general type, regardless of maker
trigger_label: Walton markets Linoleum floor covering
trigger_desc: Frederick Walton coined and registered Linoleum around 1864 for his linseed-oil floor covering. Because he did not police the term, it was held to have become generic in an 1878 English court ruling, one of the earliest recorded cases of trademark genericide.

sense_from: in a literal manner; word for word; in the actual or exact sense, without exaggeration or metaphor
sense_to: used as a non-literal intensifier to add emphasis to a statement that is not literally true; in effect, virtually, to a great degree
trigger_label: Semantic bleaching of an assertive into an intensifier
trigger_desc: There is no single datable event. The intensifier use arose gradually in literary English from the 18th century onward, as a strong assertion of truth weakened into a generic emphasiser. The anchor year is a representative attestation point, not a cause.

sense_from: a member of the 1811-1816 English textile-workers' movement that destroyed labour-saving machinery, said to follow the (legendary) Ned Ludd
sense_to: a person opposed to new technology or to technological change in general
trigger_label: Luddite machine-breaking uprisings
trigger_desc: Between 1811 and 1816 textile workers in the English Midlands and north destroyed mechanized looms and frames, fearing loss of livelihood. The movement, invoking the legendary Ned Ludd, gave its name first to the participants and later, by broadening, to any opponent of technology.

sense_from: lying press; a polemical accusation against media outlets accused of deliberate deception, used across the political spectrum
sense_to: blanket far-right slogan against mainstream media, associated with anti-democratic and violent opposition to journalism
trigger_label: PEGIDA movement demonstrations, Dresden 2014
trigger_desc: From October 2014, PEGIDA demonstrators chanted 'Luegenpresse' at journalists, reactivating a Nazi-era slur and cementing its association with the far-right. Unwort des Jahres 2014.

sense_from: the surname of the Austrian physicist and philosopher Ernst Mach
sense_to: the Mach number, the ratio of an object's speed to the local speed of sound; an everyday term for high-speed and supersonic flight
trigger_label: Mach studies supersonic shock waves
trigger_desc: Around 1887 Ernst Mach photographed shock waves around projectiles moving faster than sound and described the relationship now expressed by the Mach number. The dimensionless number was named for him by Jakob Ackeret in 1929.

sense_from: of or pertaining to Niccolo Machiavelli or to the political doctrines set out in his treatise 'Il Principe'
sense_to: cunning, scheming, and unscrupulous, especially in the pursuit and exercise of power; using deceit and expediency without regard to morality
trigger_label: Machiavelli's 'Il Principe' (1532)
trigger_desc: Niccolo Machiavelli's treatise 'Il Principe', written 1513 and printed posthumously in 1532, advised rulers that the ends of holding power could justify deceit and cruelty. Its reception, often as a byword for amoral statecraft, made 'Machiavellian' a synonym for cunning and unscrupulous scheming in English from around 1600.

sense_from: of or relating to Mrs Malaprop, the word-mangling character in Sheridan's comedy The Rivals
sense_to: the mistaken use of a word in place of a similar-sounding one, often with comic effect
trigger_label: Premiere of Sheridan's The Rivals
trigger_desc: Richard Brinsley Sheridan's comedy The Rivals premiered in 1775. Its character Mrs Malaprop habitually confused similar-sounding words; her name (from French 'mal a propos') became the term for such verbal blunders by metaphor.

sense_from: the plain of Marathon in Attica, site of the Athenian victory over Persia and of the legendary long-distance run to Athens
sense_to: a long-distance footrace of about 42 kilometres; by extension any prolonged or testing effort
trigger_label: Marathon race at the 1896 Athens Olympics
trigger_desc: The first modern Olympics of 1896 staged a long-distance race commemorating the legendary run from the plain of Marathon to Athens after the 490 BC battle. The event fixed the ancient place name as the name of the race.

sense_from: a mouse: the small rodent of genus Mus and related genera
sense_to: computer mouse: a handheld input device moved across a surface to control a screen pointer
trigger_label: Apple Macintosh launch and personal computing revolution
trigger_desc: The launch of the Apple Macintosh in 1984 and the subsequent spread of GUI-based personal computers brought the computer 'mouse' metaphor into mainstream German vocabulary.

sense_from: the monumental tomb of Mausolus, satrap of Caria, at Halicarnassus, one of the Seven Wonders of the ancient world
sense_to: any large, stately, free-standing tomb or building housing tombs
trigger_label: Construction of the tomb of Mausolus at Halicarnassus
trigger_desc: Around 353 to 351 BC a monumental tomb was built at Halicarnassus for Mausolus, satrap of Caria, and his sister-wife Artemisia. Its fame as one of the Seven Wonders made the ruler's name (Greek Mausoleion) the generic word for any grand tomb.

sense_from: an unbranded calf or yearling found without an owner's mark, after rancher Samuel A. Maverick (1803-1870) who left his cattle unbranded
sense_to: an independent-minded, unconventional person who refuses to conform to a group or party
trigger_label: Samuel Maverick's unbranded Texas cattle
trigger_desc: Texas landowner Samuel A. Maverick was known for not branding his cattle, so unmarked strays came to be called 'mavericks' by the mid-1860s. The cattle sense broadened to mean any unattached, independent individual.

sense_from: Mentor, the friend of Odysseus whose form Athena assumed to guide and advise the young Telemachus in Homer's Odyssey
sense_to: a wise and trusted counsellor or teacher; an experienced person who guides and supports someone less experienced
trigger_label: Fenelon's 'Les Aventures de Telemaque' (1699)
trigger_desc: Mentor is a minor figure in Homer's Odyssey, but Francois Fenelon's enormously popular didactic romance 'Les Aventures de Telemaque' (1699) made Mentor the central wise counsellor to the young prince. The book's pan-European success turned the proper name into a common noun for a trusted adviser, attested in English from the mid eighteenth century.

sense_from: to subject to Mesmer's animal magnetism; to hypnotise
sense_to: to hold someone's attention completely; to captivate or fascinate
trigger_label: Franz Mesmer's animal magnetism in vogue in Paris
trigger_desc: In the 1780s the physician Franz Mesmer popularised 'animal magnetism' as a healing practice in Paris. The verb 'mesmerize' was formed from his name to mean 'to hypnotise', then broadened by metaphor to 'captivate, fascinate'.

sense_from: a reflective surface, typically of glass; to reflect
sense_to: a server or site holding an identical copy of another's files; to maintain such a synchronised copy
trigger_label: rise of internet FTP mirror sites
trigger_desc: As anonymous-FTP archives and software distribution grew across the early internet, popular archives were duplicated onto 'mirror' servers to share load and improve access, fixing the computing sense in the early 1990s.

sense_from: derived from the surname of the American inventor and painter Samuel F. B. Morse
sense_to: a method of encoding text as standardized sequences of short and long signals (dots and dashes); an everyday term for the dot-dash signalling system
trigger_label: Morse and Vail develop the telegraph code
trigger_desc: From 1838 Samuel Morse and Alfred Vail developed the signalling code for the electric telegraph, publicly demonstrated in 1838 and used on the first US telegraph line in 1844. The code became known by Morse's name.

sense_from: a mouse: the small rodent of genus Mus and related genera
sense_to: computer mouse: a handheld input device moved across a surface to control a screen pointer
trigger_label: Apple Macintosh launch and personal computing revolution
trigger_desc: The launch of the Apple Macintosh in 1984 and the subsequent spread of GUI-based personal computers brought the computer 'mouse' metaphor into mainstream German vocabulary.

sense_from: the condition of Narcissus, the youth of Greek myth who pined away in love with his own reflected image
sense_to: excessive self-love or self-admiration; in psychology, a personality pattern of grandiosity, need for admiration, and lack of empathy
trigger_label: Coinage of 'Narcissism' as a clinical term
trigger_desc: Havelock Ellis in 1898 compared a form of self-absorbed sexuality to the myth of Narcissus; in 1899 the German psychiatrist Paul Naecke coined 'Narzissmus' in 'Die sexuellen Perversitaeten'. The term entered English by 1905 and Freud's later work fixed its clinical sense, turning the mythic figure into a psychological category.

sense_from: wicked, morally bad, given to vice or evil; (earlier) having naught, poor, needy (from 'naught', nothing)
sense_to: mischievous, disobedient (esp. of a child); mildly improper or risque, but not gravely wicked
trigger_label: Gradual weakening of a moral condemnation
trigger_desc: No single datable event. The strong moral sense 'wicked' weakened over the 17th century to the milder 'mischievous, disobedient', a common softening of moral terms when applied colloquially and to children. The anchor year is a representative attestation, not a cause.

sense_from: Nemesis, the Greek goddess who dispensed retribution against those guilty of hubris or undeserved good fortune; divine vengeance
sense_to: an inescapable agent of one's downfall; a persistent and formidable rival or opponent who cannot be overcome
trigger_label: Greek goddess Nemesis
trigger_desc: Nemesis personified divine retribution in Greek religion, punishing arrogance and the disturbance of due proportion. Adopted into English as the name of the goddess, the word generalized to mean an instrument of inescapable downfall and then an unbeatable adversary.

sense_from: a net or mesh of knotted cord for catching fish or game; any interwoven network
sense_to: the internet / the World Wide Web (das Netz, im Netz)
trigger_label: World Wide Web and Mosaic browser launch
trigger_desc: The public launch of the WWW and graphical browsers (Mosaic 1993, Netscape 1994) popularised 'surfing the internet'; Jean Armour Polly's 1992 article coined the phrase in English, which entered German as 'im Internet surfen'.

sense_from: derived from the surname of the French diplomat Jean Nicot, via the tobacco genus Nicotiana named for him
sense_to: the addictive alkaloid present in tobacco; an everyday word for the active and habit-forming component of cigarettes
trigger_label: Nicot introduces tobacco to the French court
trigger_desc: In 1560 Jean Nicot, French ambassador to Portugal, sent tobacco plants and seeds to the court in Paris. The plant genus Nicotiana was named for him, and the alkaloid isolated in 1828 took the name nicotine.

sense_from: publicly or generally known; well-known, manifest; a neutral statement of being widely recognised (Medieval Latin notorius)
sense_to: widely and unfavourably known; famous for something bad; infamous, of ill repute
trigger_label: Pejoration by negative collocation
trigger_desc: No single datable event. 'Notorious' was repeatedly applied to bad or scandalous matters (notorious offenders, notorious crimes), and the negative collocations transferred their colour to the word over the late 16th century. The anchor year is a representative attestation, not a cause.

sense_from: DuPont's coined product name for its new synthetic polyamide fibre
sense_to: any synthetic polyamide polymer; fabric or stockings made from it
trigger_label: DuPont announces nylon
trigger_desc: In 1938 DuPont publicly announced nylon, a coined name for its new synthetic polyamide fibre, deliberately left as a generic descriptor rather than a registered trademark. It rapidly became the ordinary word for the polymer and the products made from it.

sense_from: the surname of the German physicist Georg Simon Ohm
sense_to: the SI unit of electrical resistance
trigger_label: Ohm publishes Ohm's law
trigger_desc: In 1827 Georg Simon Ohm published 'Die galvanische Kette, mathematisch bearbeitet', stating the proportionality of current and voltage. His name became the unit of resistance, adopted by the British Association in the 1860s and standardized internationally.

sense_from: the surname of the German physicist Georg Simon Ohm
sense_to: the SI unit of electrical resistance; an everyday word for resistance to current flow
trigger_label: Ohm publishes Ohm's law
trigger_desc: In 1827 Georg Simon Ohm published 'Die galvanische Kette, mathematisch bearbeitet', stating the proportionality of current and voltage. His name was adopted for the unit of resistance by the British Association in the 1860s.

sense_from: of or pertaining to George Orwell or to his writings and political views
sense_to: characteristic of the repressive, manipulative totalitarian state depicted in Orwell's fiction: pervasive surveillance, propaganda, doublethink, and the distortion of truth
trigger_label: Publication of Orwell's 'Nineteen Eighty-Four' (1949)
trigger_desc: George Orwell's novel 'Nineteen Eighty-Four', published in 1949, portrayed a surveillance state of perpetual war, thought control, and falsified history. Its immediate impact, deepened by Orwell's death in 1950, turned 'Orwellian' from a mere authorial adjective into a byword for oppressive state manipulation.

sense_from: of or pertaining to the god Pan; the sudden, contagious fear that Pan was held to cause in flocks, herds, and people in wild or lonely places
sense_to: a sudden overpowering fright, often without clear cause and spreading through a crowd; an extreme uncontrolled anxiety
trigger_label: Greek myth of the god Pan and 'panic' fear
trigger_desc: In Greek myth the god Pan inhabited wild and lonely places and was held to inspire a sudden, irrational, contagious terror, the 'panikon deima'. Through Greek and Latin the adjective passed into English as 'panic', and the standalone noun for sudden fright emerged by the early eighteenth century.

sense_from: derived from the surname of the French chemist and microbiologist Louis Pasteur
sense_to: to heat a food or drink to a controlled temperature for a set time to destroy pathogens without changing it much; a routine word in dairy and food processing
trigger_label: Pasteur develops controlled heat treatment of liquids
trigger_desc: Around 1864 Louis Pasteur showed that gentle heating of wine and beer killed spoilage microbes without ruining the product. The process and the verb derived from his name spread once the germ theory of disease was accepted.

sense_from: a secular cleric; a (Roman Catholic) priest; neutral occupational term for a member of the clergy
sense_to: a priest or clergyman (contemptuous); a disparaging designation for a cleric seen as worldly, corrupt, or meddlesome
trigger_label: Protestant Reformation in the German lands
trigger_desc: The Reformation beginning 1517 sharply increased anticlericalism. Criticism of worldly, pluralist, and corrupt clergy made 'Pfaffe' a vehicle for contempt, fixing the pejorative sense.

sense_from: one of the orations of Demosthenes against Philip II of Macedon, or of Cicero against Mark Antony in imitation of them
sense_to: a bitter verbal attack or denunciation; an impassioned invective speech
trigger_label: Demosthenes' Philippics against Philip II (from 351 BC)
trigger_desc: From 351 BC the Athenian orator Demosthenes delivered a series of vehement speeches urging resistance to Philip II of Macedon; these were called the 'Philippics'. Cicero later borrowed the name for his attacks on Mark Antony. The proper name generalized to mean any bitter denunciatory oration.

sense_from: a member of the Philistines, the Biblical people of the coastal plain who fought the Israelites
sense_to: in student slang, a townsman or non-student; hence a narrow-minded, unintellectual, complacently materialistic person
trigger_label: German university student slang, late 17th century
trigger_desc: In German university towns students adopted 'Philister' as a label for townsmen and non-students, opposing themselves to the Biblical enemies of the chosen people. A 1693 Jena funeral sermon for a student killed by townsmen is a frequently cited early attestation. The sense broadened to the narrow-minded, unintellectual person.

sense_from: cunning, crafty, wily; (later) clever, skilful, ingenious (Old English praettig, from praett 'a trick')
sense_to: as an adverb: to a fair or moderate degree; fairly, rather, moderately (a downtoner, as in 'pretty good')
trigger_label: Bleaching of a degree adverb into a downtoner
trigger_desc: No single datable event. The adverbial 'pretty' weakened gradually from 'in a clever or fine manner' to a moderating downtoner over the 16th-17th centuries, a routine path for degree adverbs. The anchor year is the earliest adverbial attestation, not a cause.

sense_from: the propertyless lowest census class of ancient Rome, contributing nothing to the state but offspring
sense_to: the modern industrial working class that owns no means of production and lives by selling its labour; a collective political subject
trigger_label: 1840s socialist movement and the Communist Manifesto (1848)
trigger_desc: In the 1840s the early socialist movement, and the Communist Manifesto of 1848, revived 'Proletariat' for the modern industrial working class and recast it from a Roman class of paupers into a positively charged collective political subject.

sense_from: the propagation of (the Catholic) faith; the spreading of doctrines or beliefs; a neutral term for dissemination
sense_to: biased or misleading information used to promote a political cause or point of view; manipulative mass persuasion
trigger_label: Founding of the Congregatio de Propaganda Fide
trigger_desc: In 1622 Pope Gregory XV established the Congregatio de Propaganda Fide to coordinate Catholic missionary activity during the Counter-Reformation. The ecclesiastical noun 'propaganda' was carried over into secular political discourse, where it acquired its negative sense of manipulative persuasion.

sense_from: a span of forty days; a generic count of forty (Italian quarantina, from quaranta '40')
sense_to: enforced isolation of persons, ships, or goods to prevent the spread of contagious disease
trigger_label: Venetian and Ragusan plague isolation regime
trigger_desc: In 1377 the Great Council of Ragusa (Dubrovnik) imposed a thirty-day (trentina) isolation on arrivals from plague areas; Venice and other ports extended it to forty days (quarantina). The fixed forty-day ship isolation against the Black Death attached the disease-control meaning to the number-word.

sense_from: derogatory slur targeting homosexual or gender-nonconforming people
sense_to: umbrella term for non-heterosexual and non-cisgender identities, reclaimed as neutral or positive by the LGBTQ+ community
trigger_label: Queer Nation and ACT UP activist reappropriation
trigger_desc: Queer Nation (founded 1990) and ACT UP activists deliberately reclaimed 'queer' as an empowering self-description, reversing its slur status.

sense_from: a creative non-conformist; someone who thinks outside conventional lines
sense_to: a participant in anti-lockdown / COVID-conspiracy protests
trigger_label: Querdenken 711 protest movement
trigger_desc: Anti-lockdown protest movement founded in Stuttgart in 2020, which fixed the new pejorative reading of the word.

sense_from: a creative non-conformist; someone who thinks outside conventional lines
sense_to: a participant in anti-lockdown / COVID-conspiracy protests
trigger_label: COVID-19 pandemic

sense_from: the surname of Vidkun Quisling (1887-1945), Norwegian politician who collaborated with Nazi Germany
sense_to: a traitor who collaborates with an occupying enemy power; a fifth columnist
trigger_label: German invasion of Norway and Quisling's coup attempt
trigger_desc: On 9 April 1940 Germany invaded Norway; Vidkun Quisling proclaimed himself head of government. Within weeks Allied press, notably a 1940 leader in The Times, turned his name into a generic term for traitor.

sense_from: of or relating to Don Quixote, the deluded knight-errant of Cervantes' novel
sense_to: extravagantly idealistic and unrealistic; pursuing lofty but impractical goals
trigger_label: Publication of Cervantes' Don Quixote
trigger_desc: The first part of Miguel de Cervantes' novel Don Quixote was published in 1605. The hero's idealistic but deluded chivalry made his name a byword for romantic impracticality, yielding the adjective 'quixotic' through metaphor.

sense_from: an artificial manufactured person or humanoid worker, as in Capek's play R.U.R.
sense_to: any automated machine or autonomous device that performs tasks, often programmable
trigger_label: Coining of 'robot' in Karel Capek's play R.U.R.
trigger_desc: Karel Capek's 1920 play R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots) introduced 'robot', from Czech 'robota' (forced labour). The neologism rapidly spread and broadened to mechanical and automated devices.

sense_from: surname of the physicist Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen (1845-1923)
sense_to: the X-rays discovered by Roentgen; by extension X-ray imaging and the act of taking such an image (German 'roentgen', verb)
trigger_label: Roentgen's discovery of X-rays at Wuerzburg
trigger_desc: On 8 November 1895 Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen discovered a new kind of radiation at the University of Wuerzburg. In German the rays and the resulting imaging were named after him (Roentgenstrahlen, roentgen), turning the surname into a generic medical and technical term.

sense_from: Rugby School, the public school in Warwickshire where the handling game of football developed
sense_to: the football code played by handling and carrying an oval ball, with tackling and scrummaging
trigger_label: Handling code at Rugby School
trigger_desc: The distinctive handling game of football was associated with Rugby School from the 1820s. As football codes diverged, the school name was adopted for the handling code and its game.

sense_from: the deliberate slowing or bungling of work by labourers as an industrial weapon
sense_to: any deliberate destruction, obstruction, or undermining of an effort, machine, or cause
trigger_label: French syndicalist promotion of 'sabotage' as industrial action
trigger_desc: Around 1910 the French syndicalist movement (CGT) openly advocated 'sabotage' as a tactic, popularising the word; English borrowed it in this narrow labour sense before broadening it during and after the First World War.

sense_from: the title of John Montagu, 4th Earl of Sandwich, an English nobleman
sense_to: two or more slices of bread with a filling between them, eaten as a light meal
trigger_label: John Montagu, 4th Earl of Sandwich, popularises bread-and-filling meals
trigger_desc: Around 1762 John Montagu, 4th Earl of Sandwich, was reported to order meat served between bread so he could eat without leaving the gaming table. His title became attached to the food, producing the modern food sense via metonymy.

sense_from: formed from the surname of the Belgian instrument maker Adolphe Sax plus the Greek element -phone 'sound'
sense_to: a single-reed woodwind instrument with a conical brass body; an everyday word central to jazz and popular music
trigger_label: Sax patents the saxophone
trigger_desc: In 1846 Adolphe Sax obtained a French patent for the family of instruments he had designed. The instruments and the word formed from his name spread rapidly through military and later jazz ensembles.

sense_from: a defensive trench dug to shelter and conceal riflemen; a fieldwork fortification term
sense_to: the emblematic trench of the First World War; figuratively, an entrenched and irreconcilable position in a conflict
trigger_label: First World War trench warfare on the Western Front 1914-1918
trigger_desc: After the front stalemated in late 1914, the war became a war of trenches. 'Schuetzengraben' became the emblematic word of the First World War (1914-1918) and later a figure for entrenched, irreconcilable positions.

sense_from: homosexual (of a man), used contemptuously; a derogatory designation
sense_to: gay: a neutral or positive self-identifier for homosexual men after reappropriation
trigger_label: Stonewall Riots and gay liberation movement
trigger_desc: The 1969 Stonewall Riots and subsequent gay liberation movement accelerated the reappropriation of 'gay' as a neutral/positive self-identifier.

sense_from: someone who speaks or writes in an incoherent, muddled, or verbose manner
sense_to: a conspiracy theorist; specifically someone spreading anti-vaccination or COVID-denying pseudoscience; a COVID sceptic or far-right health-freedom activist
trigger_label: COVID-19 pandemic and Querdenken protests in Germany
trigger_desc: The COVID-19 pandemic and associated anti-vaccination and lockdown-sceptic movements, including Querdenken, drove the narrowing of Schwurbler to a specifically political pejorative label for conspiracy theorists.

sense_from: the spherical case shot named after its inventor Henry Shrapnel: a shell packed with bullets that bursts in mid-air
sense_to: fragments thrown out by any exploding shell, bomb, or device; loosely, any small flying debris from an explosion
trigger_label: Shrapnel develops the bursting case shot
trigger_desc: From 1784 Henry Shrapnel developed a shell filled with shot that burst in flight; the British Army adopted it in 1803 and it carried his name. In World War One the word broadened to mean shell fragments in general.

sense_from: ill, unwell, affected by disease; (also, later) morally disturbing, perverse, in poor taste
sense_to: (slang) excellent, impressive, awesome; remarkably good or skilful
trigger_label: Youth-culture slang reversal of a negative term
trigger_desc: No single datable event. Like 'bad', 'wicked' and 'ill', 'sick' was reversed to a term of approval in late 20th-century youth slang (skate, surf, hip hop), where extremity itself is praised. The anchor year is a representative attestation, not a cause.

sense_from: the surname of Etienne de Silhouette, French controller-general of finances in 1759, associated with cheap, austere economy
sense_to: a dark profile portrait or outline shape filled in with a solid colour; the outline of an object seen against lighter background
trigger_label: Tenure of Etienne de Silhouette as controller-general of finances
trigger_desc: Etienne de Silhouette served briefly as French controller-general of finances in 1759. His name became a byword for cheapness and austerity, and was attached to the inexpensive shadow-outline portraits then in fashion, from which the general sense of an outline shape developed.

sense_from: adulterated, falsified, tampered with; mixed with a foreign or inferior substance; no longer pure or genuine
sense_to: refined, cultured, worldly-wise; free of naivety; (of a system or device) complex, advanced, highly developed
trigger_label: Revaluation of worldliness from corruption to refinement
trigger_desc: No single datable event. The loss of naive purity, once a defect (adulteration), was gradually reinterpreted as the positive quality of worldly refinement over the 19th century. The anchor year marks the established positive sense, not a cause.

sense_from: the town of Spa in the Ardennes (modern Belgium), renowned for its curative mineral springs
sense_to: any mineral spring or resort, health establishment, or bathing facility offering such waters or treatments
trigger_label: Fame of Spa's mineral springs
trigger_desc: The Belgian town of Spa became the leading European destination for mineral-water cures. English visitors generalised the place name to mean any such spring resort, and later any health and bathing establishment.

sense_from: SPAM: Hormel Foods brand name for a canned cooked pork product
sense_to: unsolicited bulk messages, especially commercial email; unwanted repetitive online content
trigger_label: Monty Python's Flying Circus 'Spam' sketch (1970)
trigger_desc: The 1970 Monty Python sketch featuring 'Spam' repeated ad nauseam embedded the word's association with unwanted repetitive content in English geek culture, which internet users later applied to bulk email.

sense_from: SPAM: Hormel Foods brand name for a canned cooked pork product
sense_to: unsolicited bulk messages, especially commercial email; unwanted repetitive online content
trigger_label: Usenet bulk posting incidents (early 1990s)
trigger_desc: Early Usenet flooding incidents (notably Canter and Siegel's 1994 bulk posting) were labelled 'spamming' by analogy with the Monty Python sketch, cementing the digital sense.

sense_from: of or belonging to Sparta or its citizens in ancient Greece
sense_to: marked by austerity, severe self-discipline, frugality, and the avoidance of comfort or luxury
trigger_label: Reputation of Spartan austerity
trigger_desc: Ancient accounts of Sparta's harsh military upbringing and plain living made the civic name a byword for austerity. The adjective generalised to mean severe simplicity and frugal endurance.

sense_from: to reflect (as in a mirror); to mirror or be reflected
sense_to: to mirror data: to maintain an identical synchronised copy of files on another server or disk
trigger_label: rise of internet FTP mirror sites
trigger_desc: As anonymous-FTP archives and software distribution grew across the early internet, popular archives were duplicated onto 'mirror' servers to share load and improve access, fixing the computing sense in the early 1990s.

sense_from: a townsman serving in the civic militia, armed with a pike; a full citizen of a medieval or early-modern town
sense_to: a narrow-minded, conventional, philistine person; a smug conformist hostile to anything unfamiliar
trigger_label: Student slang and 19th c. critique of the bourgeoisie
trigger_desc: From the 18th c. students used 'Spiessbuerger' to mock the settled townsman as the opposite of the free academic. The 19th c. intellectual and artistic critique of bourgeois conformism fixed the short form 'Spiesser' as a pejorative for the narrow, philistine conformist.

sense_from: the obsessive, ultimately violent fan in Eminem's 2000 song 'Stan' (proper name)
sense_to: an overzealous or obsessive fan; (verb) to be an enthusiastic and devoted fan of someone
trigger_label: Eminem's song 'Stan' (2000)
trigger_desc: Eminem's 2000 song 'Stan', about a fan whose devotion turns deadly, gave its name to the common noun for an obsessive fan; social-media fandom later generalised it into the verb 'to stan'.

sense_from: a small flowing body of water; a continuous flow of liquid, air, or people
sense_to: to transmit or receive audio or video as a continuous flow over the internet for real-time playback without prior download
trigger_label: rise of broadband streaming media services
trigger_desc: Broadband internet and services such as YouTube (2005), Netflix streaming (2007) and Spotify (2008) made consuming audio and video as a continuous online flow mainstream, fixing the verb 'to stream' in the media sense.

sense_from: to transmit data as a continuous flow (technical computing sense)
sense_to: to stream: to play audio or video as a continuous online flow without prior download
trigger_label: rise of broadband streaming media services
trigger_desc: Broadband internet and services such as YouTube (2005), Netflix streaming (2007) and Spotify (2008) made consuming audio and video as a continuous online flow mainstream, fixing the verb 'to stream' in the media sense.

sense_from: an early loan from English 'strike', a stoppage or refusal of work, not yet a fixed German technical term
sense_to: an organised collective stoppage of work by employees to enforce demands; the central instrument of the labour movement
trigger_label: German labour movement and trade unions, 1860s-1870s
trigger_desc: From the 1860s the rising German labour movement and the founding of trade unions fixed the English loan 'Streik' as the technical term for an organised collective stoppage of work to enforce demands.

sense_from: to surf: to ride ocean waves on a surfboard (sporting activity)
sense_to: to surf (the internet / the web): to browse the World Wide Web, navigating from page to page
trigger_label: World Wide Web and Mosaic browser launch
trigger_desc: The public launch of the WWW and graphical browsers (Mosaic 1993, Netscape 1994) popularised 'surfing the internet'; Jean Armour Polly's 1992 article coined the phrase in English, which entered German as 'im Internet surfen'.

sense_from: to surf: to ride ocean waves on a surfboard (sporting activity)
sense_to: to surf (the internet): to browse the World Wide Web, navigating from page to page
trigger_label: World Wide Web and Mosaic browser launch
trigger_desc: The public launch of the WWW and graphical browsers (Mosaic 1993, Netscape 1994) popularised 'surfing the internet'; Jean Armour Polly's 1992 article coined the phrase in English, which entered German as 'im Internet surfen'.

sense_from: informal clipping of 'suspicious' or 'suspect' (chiefly British/police slang)
sense_to: (internet slang) questionable, dubious, or untrustworthy; arousing suspicion
trigger_label: Among Us popularity surge (2020)
trigger_desc: The social-deduction game Among Us, released in 2018, exploded in popularity during the 2020 lockdowns; players accusing each other of being 'sus' (suspicious) propelled the clipped form into mainstream internet slang.

sense_from: a large receptacle or storage container for liquid or gas
sense_to: an armoured, tracked military fighting vehicle
trigger_label: British 'tank' codename and combat debut on the Somme
trigger_desc: In 1915-1916 the British used 'tank' as a secrecy codename, disguising the new armoured vehicle as a water carrier. Its combat debut at the Somme in September 1916 fixed the military sense in public language.

sense_from: to subject to the punishment of Tantalus, who in Greek myth stood in water and beneath fruit that withdrew whenever he reached for them
sense_to: to tease or torment by presenting something desirable while keeping it out of reach; to excite a hope that cannot be satisfied
trigger_label: Greek myth of Tantalus
trigger_desc: In Greek myth Tantalus was condemned in the underworld to stand in a pool beneath fruit-laden boughs, both of which drew away whenever he reached to drink or eat.
