The verb 'tantalize' was coined in late sixteenth-century English directly from his name to mean teasing with the unattainable.

sense_from: the Tempo brand of disposable paper handkerchief
sense_to: any disposable paper tissue, regardless of maker (colloquial German)
trigger_label: Tempo paper handkerchief brand registered
trigger_desc: In 1929 the Vereinigte Papierwerke registered the Tempo brand of disposable paper handkerchief at the Reichspatentamt in Berlin. The brand became the colloquial German word for any paper tissue.

sense_from: causing terror; frightful, dreadful; provoking fear (from Latin terrificus, terrere 'to frighten')
sense_to: excellent, wonderful, splendid; remarkably good; (also) very great in degree
trigger_label: Hyperbolic bleaching of a fear word into praise
trigger_desc: No single datable event. Like 'awful', 'tremendous' and 'dreadful', the word weakened through colloquial hyperbole from 'frightful' to 'very great' to 'excellent' over the 19th century. The anchor year marks the colloquial positive attestation, not a cause.

sense_from: the Thermos brand of vacuum flask for keeping liquids hot or cold
sense_to: any vacuum flask, regardless of maker
trigger_label: Thermos brand vacuum flask launched
trigger_desc: In 1904 the brand Thermos was adopted for the commercialised vacuum flask (based on James Dewar's earlier invention). The brand spread so widely that the word came to mean any vacuum flask, and a US court ruled it generic in 1963.

sense_from: mad, reckless, rabid; out of one's mind; wild and uncontrolled
sense_to: great, fantastic, wonderful, impressive (colloquial positive)
trigger_label: Post-war West German youth culture and rock music
trigger_desc: The emergence of rock and roll in West Germany in the late 1950s/early 1960s led young people to resemanticize 'toll' (the wild-ones/tolle quality) from a negative to an enthusiastically positive colloquial marker.

sense_from: a troll: a mythological being of Scandinavian folklore
sense_to: an internet troll: a person who posts deliberately provocative messages online
trigger_label: Usenet 'trolling for newbies' coinage (early 1990s)
trigger_desc: On Usenet around 1992, 'trolling' (from the fishing sense of trailing bait) named the practice of posting provocative messages to bait responses; the agent noun 'troll' followed and broadened across the social web.

sense_from: to fish by trailing a baited line; also the folkloric cave-dwelling monster of Norse origin
sense_to: a person who posts deliberately provocative, inflammatory, or off-topic messages online to upset others or derail discussion
trigger_label: Usenet 'trolling for newbies' coinage (early 1990s)
trigger_desc: On Usenet around 1992, 'trolling' (from the fishing sense of trailing bait) named the practice of posting provocative messages to bait responses; the agent noun 'troll' followed and broadened across the social web.

sense_from: sustained, very rapid artillery fire so dense the impacts merge into a drumming roar
sense_to: a relentless, overwhelming barrage of words, questions, accusations, or criticism
trigger_label: First World War material battles, Verdun and the Somme 1916
trigger_desc: The great material battles of 1916 (Verdun, the Somme) made sustained barrage fire a defining experience of the First World War. The artillery term 'Trommelfeuer' passed into figurative use for any relentless verbal barrage.

sense_from: guinea fowl imported into Europe through Turkish (Ottoman) territory, originally called turkey-cock or turkey-hen
sense_to: the large North American gallinaceous bird, Meleagris gallopavo, raised for food
trigger_label: Confusion of guinea fowl with the American bird
trigger_desc: Guinea fowl reached England through Turkish merchants and were called 'turkey'. When the larger New World bird arrived in the 16th century it was conflated with that fowl and inherited the country name.

sense_from: Tuxedo Park, the wealthy resort in New York state where the dinner jacket was first worn
sense_to: a man's semiformal evening jacket; by extension the full dinner-dress outfit
trigger_label: Dinner jacket worn at Tuxedo Park
trigger_desc: The semiformal evening jacket was first worn at the Tuxedo Park country club around 1886. The resort name attached to the garment and entered general use by 1889.

sense_from: a short, high-pitched sound made by a small bird; to make such a sound
sense_to: a post or message published on the Twitter / X social media platform; to post such a message
trigger_label: Launch of Twitter social media platform (2006)
trigger_desc: Twitter launched in 2006 with a deliberate bird metaphor; users organically adopted 'tweet' for a post by 2007, which Twitter later formalised in its UI.

sense_from: August Fischer's brand of clear synthetic-resin adhesive
sense_to: loosely, any clear tube glue (colloquial German)
trigger_label: Fischer markets Uhu adhesive
trigger_desc: In 1932 the pharmacist August Fischer of Buehl presented the first ready-to-use clear synthetic-resin adhesive under the brand Uhu (named after the eagle-owl). The brand became a widely used colloquial label for clear tube glue.

sense_from: (archaic, rare) to make unfriendly or estrange; as a noun, an enemy
sense_to: to remove someone from one's list of friends or contacts on a social-networking site
trigger_label: Facebook 'friend' / 'unfriend' interface (mid-2000s)
trigger_desc: Facebook's friends model, opened to the general public in 2006, gave 'friend' and 'unfriend' precise platform meanings; 'unfriend' was named New Oxford American Dictionary Word of the Year in 2009.

sense_from: of or relating to the Vandals, a Germanic people of late antiquity who sacked Rome in 455
sense_to: wilful or malicious destruction or defacement of property, art, or public works
trigger_label: Henri Gregoire coins 'vandalisme' during the French Revolution
trigger_desc: In 1794 the abbe Henri Gregoire coined the French term 'vandalisme' to denounce the revolutionary destruction of monuments and artworks, invoking the Vandals' reputation for sacking Rome. The word spread into German as 'Vandalismus' with its negative sense.

sense_from: of, relating to, or caused by a virus; virology adjective
sense_to: spreading rapidly and widely through the internet or social networks, like a virus
trigger_label: rise of the World Wide Web and email forwarding culture
trigger_desc: The 1990s web popularised the concept of content spreading exponentially like a biological virus. The term 'viral marketing' (Jurvetson/Draper 1997) anchored the metaphor.

sense_from: an infectious agent that replicates inside living cells; figuratively, a harmful influence
sense_to: computer virus: a self-replicating malicious program that attaches itself to files or programs and spreads between computers
trigger_label: naming of the computer virus and early PC infections
trigger_desc: Fred Cohen defined and named the 'computer virus' in 1983-1984 academic work; the Brain boot-sector virus (1986) and the spread of personal computers carried the term into mainstream English.

sense_from: an infectious biological agent that replicates inside living cells
sense_to: computer virus: a self-replicating malicious program that spreads between computers
trigger_label: naming of the computer virus and early PC infections
trigger_desc: Fred Cohen defined and named the 'computer virus' in 1983-1984 academic work; the Brain boot-sector virus (1986) and the spread of personal computers carried the term into mainstream English.

sense_from: the surname of the Italian physicist Alessandro Volta
sense_to: the SI unit of electric potential difference and electromotive force; an everyday word for electrical 'pressure'
trigger_label: Volta invents the voltaic pile
trigger_desc: In 1800 Alessandro Volta announced the voltaic pile, the first continuous source of electric current. His name was later attached to the unit of electric potential, adopted internationally in the 1880s.

sense_from: madness, insanity; a serious mental derangement
sense_to: (colloquial exclamation) amazing, incredible, fantastic; an expression of admiration or astonishment
trigger_label: Umgangssprachliche Aufwertung eines Negativworts zum Ausruf
trigger_desc: Kein einzelnes datierbares Ereignis. 'Wahnsinn' verblasste umgangssprachlich von 'Irrsinn' zum positiven Ausruf der Bewunderung, aehnlich wie 'irre' oder 'verrueckt'. Das Ankerjahr ist eine repraesentative Belegstelle, keine Ursache.

sense_from: the surname of the Scottish engineer James Watt
sense_to: the SI unit of power; an everyday word for the power rating of a device such as a bulb or appliance
trigger_label: Watt's improved steam engine
trigger_desc: From 1776 James Watt's separate-condenser steam engine entered commercial use, transforming industry. His name was adopted for the unit of power by the British Association in 1882.

sense_from: a network of fine threads spun by a spider; any interwoven or interconnected structure
sense_to: the World Wide Web: the interlinked system of hypertext documents accessed over the internet
trigger_label: World Wide Web and Mosaic browser launch
trigger_desc: The public launch of the WWW and graphical browsers (Mosaic 1993, Netscape 1994) popularised 'surfing the internet'; Jean Armour Polly's 1992 article coined the phrase in English, which entered German as 'im Internet surfen'.

sense_from: a turn, turning point, or reversal; change of direction (general)
sense_to: the (German) Wende: the political transformation of 1989/90, encompassing the fall of the Berlin Wall, the end of the GDR, and German reunification
trigger_label: Fall of the Berlin Wall (Mauerfall)
trigger_desc: The opening of the Berlin Wall on 9 November 1989 and the subsequent collapse of the GDR gave 'die Wende' its specific historical referent.

sense_from: alert to racial injustice and discrimination; socially conscious (African-American vernacular)
sense_to: broadly aware of social inequalities including race, gender, and class; associated with progressive activism
trigger_label: Black Lives Matter movement
trigger_desc: The BLM movement, especially after 2013, mainstreamed 'woke' as a marker of social-justice awareness.

sense_from: broadly aware of social inequalities including race, gender, and class; associated with progressive activism
sense_to: dismissive label for perceived excessive political correctness or identity-politics activism; used ironically or as a slur
trigger_label: US culture-war polarisation (2019-)
trigger_desc: Conservative politicians and media adopted 'woke' as a derisive label for progressive excess, completing the reversal.

sense_from: a visible mass of condensed water vapour floating in the atmosphere
sense_to: cloud computing: remote networked computing resources accessed via the internet
trigger_label: Amazon Web Services launch and popularisation of cloud computing (2006)
trigger_desc: Amazon's launch of AWS EC2 and S3 in 2006, followed by Eric Schmidt's use of 'cloud computing' at the 2006 Search Engine Strategies conference, anchored the term in mainstream technology discourse.

sense_from: an older, affluent, formerly civic-minded citizen who turns to furious protest against specific political projects such as Stuttgart 21
sense_to: any citizen expressing diffuse, sometimes irrational rage against the political establishment; increasingly pejorative
trigger_label: Spiegel essay 'Der Wutbuerger' by Dirk Kurbjuweit
trigger_desc: The essay in Der Spiegel Issue 41 of 11 October 2010 coined the term and made it immediately prominent, tying it to Stuttgart 21 protests.

sense_from: the Xerox brand of xerographic photocopying machine made by the Haloid Xerox company
sense_to: to photocopy; a photocopy made on any machine, regardless of manufacturer
trigger_label: Xerox launches the 914 copier
trigger_desc: In 1959 Haloid Xerox launched the Xerox 914, the first successful plain-paper office copier. The brand became so identified with photocopying that 'xerox' entered general use as a verb and noun, a usage the company actively discouraged.

sense_from: one of the filthy, degraded, human-shaped brutes governed by the rational horses (Houyhnhnms) in Swift's 'Gulliver's Travels'
sense_to: a coarse, rowdy, or uncouth person; a loutish boor
trigger_label: Swift's 'Gulliver's Travels' (1726)
trigger_desc: In Jonathan Swift's satire 'Gulliver's Travels' (1726) the Yahoos are vicious, filthy creatures of human form, contrasted with the rational Houyhnhnms. Swift coined the name; it passed into general English as a term of contempt for a brutish or loutish person.

sense_from: the family name of Graf Ferdinand von Zeppelin (1838-1917), German general and airship pioneer
sense_to: a rigid lighter-than-air airship; by extension any large dirigible
trigger_label: First flight of the airship LZ 1 over Lake Constance
trigger_desc: On 2 July 1900 the first Zeppelin rigid airship, LZ 1, made its maiden flight near Friedrichshafen. The inventor's name became the everyday term for the airship type.

sense_from: B.F. Goodrich's brand name for rubber galoshes closed by a hookless slide fastener
sense_to: a slide fastener with interlocking teeth, used on clothing, bags, and other goods
trigger_label: B.F. Goodrich markets the Zipper boot
trigger_desc: In 1923 the B.F. Goodrich Company adopted the name Zipper for rubber boots fitted with a hookless slide fastener. The catchy brand name was quickly transferred from the boots to the fastener, becoming the generic English word for the device.
